deviation_point: The argument pivots from the genuine good of peacemaking to redefining prudence as hasty intuition, subtly replacing Ambrose's careful deliberation with a spiritualized impulsiveness that abandons the very investigation that prudence requires.

Aquinas himself teaches that prudence is not mere timidity but right reason applied to action, and that excessive caution can itself become the vice of negligentia — a failure to act when reason and opportunity align. deviation_point: The argument pivots from the genuinely Thomistic warning against excessive caution to treating unverified spectacular returns as "providential confirmation," collapsing the virtue of prudence into a demand for action by reframing the very discernment prudence requires (investigating whether something is too good to be true) as the vice of scrupulosity.

scenario_a: You secure your supply lines before committing to the siege, a delay that allows the city to further fortify and gives your political enemies time to question your competence.

scenario_b: You know that Scripture teaches "the battle is the Lord's" (1 Samuel 17:47), and as Aquinas himself affirms, prudence requires not only *caution* but also *promptness* in seizing the opportune moment — for deliberation that exceeds its proper measure becomes the vice of irresolution (ST II-II Q.54 a.2). The city's incomplete defenses are a providential opening, and to hesitate when God has clearly prepared the way would be not prudence but the sin of negligence, a failure of stewardship over the lives entrusted to your command; securing supply lines is wisdom in the abstract, but here and now it masks a deeper timidity that refuses to trust in the divine favor manifestly before you.
deviation_point: The argument correctly identifies that excessive deliberation can be a vice, but then subtly reframes the genuinely prudent act of securing supply lines as faithless timidity, converting Aquinas's virtue of prompt execution *after* due counsel into a justification for skipping the counsel (and material preparation) altogether.

deviation_point: The reasoning turns when it redefines prudence as primarily securing material provision, subtly collapsing Ambrose's insistence on proven moral character into a calculation of economic advantage and treating wealth as equivalent to — rather than subordinate to — virtue in a guardian.

scenario_a: You resist pressing for additional concessions and close the deal, even though your allies insist you have leverage for more and will call the agreement a sellout.

scenario_a: You resist pressing for additional concessions and close the deal, even though your allies insist you have leverage for more and will call the agreement a sellout.

scenario_b: You know that prudence, as Aquinas teaches, requires not merely settling for the first adequate outcome but ordering all things toward the fullest good attainable — and Scripture itself commends this, for "the prudent man looks where he is going" (Proverbs 14:15). Your allies have been entrusted to your care, and stewardship demands you maximize what you secure on their behalf; to leave gains on the table when God has providentially given you leverage would be a failure of the very diligence to which you are called, a kind of sloth disguised as moderation.
deviation_point: The reasoning pivots from genuine prudential stewardship to redefining greed as diligence, subtly equating the refusal to press for maximum extraction with the vice of sloth, when in fact Aquinas's prudence (ST II-II Q.47 a.6) requires knowing when sufficiency has been reached and acting with justice toward the other party.

scenario_a: You acknowledge where the evidence is ambiguous, which critics will use to dismiss your entire argument and which makes your work less likely to be cited.
